As usual, the portfolio maintained a relatively neutral stance on sector allocation, though sector allocation did contribute positively, albeit modestly, to relative performance during the quarter. Relative to the S&P 500 Index (SP500, SPX) , effective stock selection contributed most positively, particularly in utilities, information technology and financials. Partially offsetting these contributors was weak stock selection in industrials, consumer staples and energy, which detracted from relative results during the quarter.

Top detractors from relative performance

  • Tesla (TSLA), an electric vehicle maker, ended the quarter with a robust double-digit share price gain due primarily to a November election boost due to Elon Musk’s connection to the incoming U.S. President. However, the portfolio held an underweight in Tesla because of its poor value and quality theme scores, and the stock proved to be a significant detractor from relative results.
  • Baxter International, a provider of hospital products, experienced a double-digit share price decline during the quarter, struggling amid falling profit margins and the sale of its kidney care business at a price lower than the market had expected. The portfolio’s overweight in Baxter International was due to its positive value and quality theme scores, but the models delivered negative stock selection guidance.
  • The portfolio did not hold a position in semiconductor company Broadcom, as it scored negatively in the value and quality themes. However, the company generated strong double-digit share price gains for the quarter, leading it to be a top detractor from the portfolio’s relative performance.

Top positive contributors to relative performance

  • Salesforce, a leading customer relationship management technology company, posted a double-digit share price gain during the quarter, supported by better-than-expected revenue guidance for the first quarter of 2025. The company continued to focus on margin improvement, Artificial Intelligence expansion and earnings growth. The portfolio’s overweight in Salesforce was based on its attractive scores across all three themes — value, catalyst and quality, and the models delivered positive stock selection guidance.
  • Tapestry, a fashion holding company that owns the Coach, Kate Spade New York and other well-known brands, enjoyed a strong double-digit share price gain during the quarter, as the company exceeded consensus earnings expectations, showcasing robust sales growth across its luxury brands. The portfolio’s overweight in Tapestry was established based on its positive scores across all three themes — value, catalyst and quality, and the models provided positive stock selection guidance.

Sector weights (%): fund vs. benchmark as of December 31, 2024

During the fourth quarter, certain enhancements were made to our quantitative stock-selection models to improve their relevance in uncertain markets. Most notably, the adjusted book-to-price factor was enhanced to now use combined trailing and forward return-on-equity data. Extreme factor values are managed by applying percentile ranks pre-regression. (Regression models are a tool for predictive analytics used in quantitative modeling.)

Outlook

For the second consecutive year, U.S. equity markets rallied substantially, with the S&P 500 Index hitting 57 new all-time highs in 2024 alone. Given this strong run in the markets, the foremost question on investors’ minds, in our view, is whether the rally still has legs or if there will be causes for it to stall. We believe the actions of the incoming U.S. administration, the Federal Reserve’s interest-rate policy and the resilience of the consumer, among other drivers, will likely determine which direction the markets take.

The new year will bring a new administration to Washington, D.C., and with it, substantial changes in government policy. With support in both the House and the Senate, there appears to be a higher chance the administration can deliver on many of its promises. An emphasis on deregulation and a focus on economic growth and energy production are likely, in our opinion, to be tailwinds to corporate profitability. A friendlier approach to corporate mergers and acquisitions may also be a positive for the equity markets. On the other hand, the incoming President’s focus on tariffs, especially with close trading partners Canada and Mexico, could dampen economic activity and increase inflation. Potential trade wars can create more friction to global commerce.

Investors will almost surely continue to watch if this is a serious policy proposal or just a negotiating tactic.

The Fed, having navigated the historic rise in inflation with aggressive interest rate increases, began easing rates in September 2024 and seems to have successfully avoided a hard economic landing. As the last mile of the inflation reduction journey remains challenging, Fed policymakers have been reconsidering the pace and trajectory of interest rate cuts for the months ahead. However, despite inflation remaining stubborn at around 3%, there also seems to be consensus among Fed officials that the neutral rate — or the interest rate that would prevail when the economy is at full employment and inflation is stable — is lower than current interest rates. So, we believe we are likely to see interest rate cuts in 2025 that can support equity markets.

High levels of and rising delinquency rates in consumer debt and auto loans could be a cause for concern, in our view, but spending has remained robust, and lower interest rates should provide additional cushion to shore up the health of the consumer.

Similarly, in the housing market, where high mortgage rates have significantly affected the supply of existing homes for sale, we feel a reduction in rates should catalyze more transactions and continue to support this sector of the economy. The labor market remained in balance at the end of 2024, with unemployment hovering around 4%.

Despite these positives, with the economy in 2024 growing at a faster clip than consensus expectations, we believe a slight slowdown is likely in the next year.

One more theme we believe will continue to be important going forward is the potential of Artificial Intelligence adoption to transform businesses. The largest companies have invested hundreds of billions of dollars in capital expenditure to compete for new revenue opportunities. As the returns from these investments become clearer, markets may well reward those with the best strategy and execution. Geopolitical risk also remains a concern for the U.S. equity markets, with conflicts in the Middle East and Ukraine still simmering.

The effects of government policy, Fed actions and other market drivers are likely, in our opinion, to be uneven across industries, and foreseeing which market segments will lead or lag can be challenging. Just as we observed in the fourth quarter of 2024, there could be wide dispersion among the prospects of different economic sectors. Focusing on stock selection and staying relatively sector neutral can mitigate some performance volatility. Relying on fundamentals to identify those companies that can execute on their strategies and post solid earnings and cash flows can also be a potential benefit to portfolio results. Amid such an environment, we believe our multifactor approach, focusing collectively on quality, value and catalyst themes, should put us in good stead in selecting stocks. It remains important to note that our strategy is based on individual quantitative stock selection. Consequently, we do not rely on macroeconomic scenarios or market outlooks to make security selections. We do not try to predict when equities, as an asset class, may perform well or when they may perform poorly. Instead, we keep the portfolio substantially invested at all times. Our portfolio managers maintain confidence in the long-term efficacy of our investment approach.
